title: Search
overview: Artificial intelligence begins with search — finding a sequence of actions that leads from an initial state to a goal. From maze-solving to chess engines, search algorithms are at the heart of many AI systems.

blocks.title: In This Lecture You Will Learn
blocks.body: Search is one of the foundational problems in AI. You'll learn how agents navigate from a start state to a goal state.
bullets: What a search problem is and how to model it
Informed search: Greedy best-first and A*

blocks.title: Agents and Environments
blocks.body: An **agent** is anything that perceives its environment through sensors and acts on it through actuators. In search, an agent needs to find a sequence of actions to reach a goal.
bullets: State: a configuration of the environment
Initial state: where the agent starts
Goal state: where the agent wants to be
Actions: what the agent can do in each state
Path cost: how expensive a solution is

blocks.title: Think of it as a Maze
blocks.text: Imagine navigating a maze. Your current position is your state. The maze walls define which actions are valid. You want to find a path from the entrance (initial state) to the exit (goal state).

blocks.title: Uninformed Search
blocks.body: Uninformed search algorithms explore without any knowledge of the goal's location. They systematically explore states until the goal is found.
bullets: BFS (Breadth-First Search) — explores level by level, uses a queue, finds shortest path
DFS (Depth-First Search) — explores one branch fully, uses a stack, memory efficient

blocks.title: BFS Pseudocode
Informed Search: A*
blocks.body: A* combines the cost to reach the current state `g(n)` with an estimated cost to the goal `h(n)`. It always finds the optimal solution when the heuristic is admissible.
bullets: f(n) = g(n) + h(n) — total estimated cost
g(n) — actual cost from start to n
h(n) — heuristic: estimated cost from n to goal

keyTakeaways.text: Agents use search to navigate from start to goal states
BFS finds the shortest path, DFS uses less memory
A* is optimal when using an admissible heuristic
Minimax finds optimal play in two-player games

whatYouLearned: How to model a problem as a search problem
The difference between BFS and DFS
How A* combines cost and heuristic
How minimax works in adversarial games
